manifest.title: Gamble Battle Endless Economy — Decision Blueprint
manifest.description: A research-backed, simulation-checked economy and escalation blueprint.

query.description: Fixed reference bankroll and player-facing prices by chapter.
filters: Deterministic model output generated from the checked-in baseline and fixed seed.
metric_definitions: Standard contract price = 1-2-5 rounded band near 20% of reference bankroll.
Bold contract price = next visible 1-2-5 band above the standard option.
Prices never use the player's current bankroll.

query.sql:
WITH policy_source (policy, simulations, survival_rate, median_bankroll, median_total_earned, median_contracts_bought, mean_win_probability, mean_bet_fraction) AS (
  VALUES
    ('odds_aware', 5000, 0.7018, 3523.62, 31809.88, 28, 0.6701, 0.2917),
    ('fixed_25', 5000, 0.7024, 1396.54, 15162.99, 21, 0.6635, 0.2691),
    ('conservative', 5000, 0.8166, 1081.82, 8502.5, 13, 0.6086, 0.1843),
    ('ten_x_floor', 5000, 0.6134, 14.14, 540.66, 2, 0.4676, 0.1608),
    ('all_in', 5000, 0, 0.46, 15.63, 0, 0.746, 0.9787)
)
SELECT * FROM policy_source;
query.description: Five deterministic policy cohorts, each simulated 5,000 times across 40 chapters.
filters: Deterministic model output generated from the checked-in baseline and fixed seed.
metric_definitions: Survival rate = share of simulations completing all 40 modeled chapters.
Median total earned = median cumulative gross winnings, not ending bankroll.
Odds-aware policy uses a fractional Kelly rule clamped to a minority wager.

query.sql:
WITH unit_source (candidate, prices, price_power_cv, top_tier_doublings, decision) AS (
  VALUES
    ('Current', '1 / 2 / 3 / 4 / 5', 0.1303, 0.0, 'Keep for base units'),
    ('Moderate widening', '1 / 2 / 4 / 7 / 11', 0.2662, 1.1375, 'Reject as main sink'),
    ('Broad exponential', '1 / 3 / 10 / 30 / 100', 0.9469, 4.3219, 'Reject as main sink')
)
SELECT * FROM unit_source;
query.description: Candidate tier schedules compared against the current 1.5x per-level stat multiplier.
filters: Deterministic model output generated from the checked-in baseline and fixed seed.
metric_definitions: Price-power CV = coefficient of variation of price divided by modeled power.
Lower CV means more consistent value per gold across unit tiers.

sources.label: Riot Games — Tocker's Trials mode overview
Hadean Tactics — Eternal Rift overview and updates

cards.description: Normal fights should feel favorable but not safe.
The independent curve used for prices and content budgets.
Odds-aware policy completion rate across 40 modeled chapters.
Ten-times-price hoarding fails to become the dominant strategy.

charts.title: Player-facing price curve by chapter
charts.subtitle: Fixed 1-2-5 price bands derived from the independent reference curve; logarithmic growth is shown as exact values in the milestone table.

charts.title: Median money earned by betting policy
charts.subtitle: 5,000 simulations per policy over 40 chapters; exact survival and behavior fields remain available in the supporting table.

tables.title: Milestone price schedule
tables.subtitle: Exact player-facing prices at selected chapters.

tables.title: Base unit price candidates
tables.subtitle: Widening fixed unit tiers buys little exponential runway and worsens value consistency.

tables.title: Policy validation details
tables.subtitle: Normal strategies retain comparable survival while pathological hoarding and all-in behavior are unattractive.

blocks.body: # Gamble Battle Endless Economy — Decision Blueprint

Keep the existing **1–5 base unit prices**. They are already better aligned with current power scaling than wider fixed tiers, and even a 1–100 schedule adds only about 4.3 doublings. Put exponential spending into **chapter contracts, donor fees, scouting, and command research**.

Target normal pre-fight projections near **68% win odds**, quote payouts from those odds, and make a minority wager the normal behavior. Use a fixed reference curve—not the player's current wallet—to set prices. Every five-fight chapter should end with one mutually exclusive, identity-changing purchase that visibly escalates combat.

Riot's Tocker's Trials was not literally endless: it used 30 rounds, six bosses, three lives, solo planning without timers, a high score, and a separate Chaos layer. The useful lesson is its clear boss cadence and permission to show boards outside normal TFT constraints. The Last Flame's endless act and Hadean Tactics' Eternal Rift reinforce the value of run-specific build mutation, save/resume support, and new upgrade vocabularies after the ordinary roster loop is mature.

Gamble Battle should combine those structures with incremental-game arithmetic: a visible exponential reference curve, periodic qualitative breakpoints, and reset boundaries that preserve identity without adding permanent exponential combat power.

1. **Unit shop:** retain 1–5 prices for ordinary recruitment; later shops become donor and contract markets.
2. **Pricing:** use `3 × 1.22^(chapter−1)` as the first tuning reference, then round offers to readable 1-2-5 bands.
3. **Odds and payouts:** start with `gross payout = clamp(1.45 / projected win probability, 1.05, 4.0)`; recalibrate before production.
4. **Escalation:** one chapter contract every five fights; contracts create transformations, chains, hazards, trait rewrites, or command changes—not repeatable +1% stats.
5. **Run structure:** endless multi-session runs persist inside a run; after defeat, preserve identity/history only, not compounding power.
6. **Score:** emphasize total money earned and depth, with peak bank and biggest wager as secondary records.

The reference curve is a content budget, not a rubber band. It defines what a healthy run should be able to earn and spend by chapter. Standard contracts cost roughly 20% of that reference; bold offers occupy the next visible price band. Actual wallet size never enters the price formula.
## Unit Prices Are Not the Exponential Sink

The current unit tiers should stay cheap and legible. Raising their fixed prices distorts value faster than it creates runway. A late-game unit can instead be valuable because it is the required donor, catalyst, or identity component for a chapter contract whose fee follows the large curve.

With the selected provisional parameters, odds-aware and fixed-25% strategies both complete 40 chapters in about 70% of runs, while conservative play trades earnings for survival. A player who waits for ten times the contract price earns only about 1.7% as much as the odds-aware policy, and repeated all-in play collapses immediately.

**Chapters 1–5:** ordinary roster building, current reroll/XP prices, first low-cost contract tutorial.

**Chapters 6–15:** contracts add first qualitative mutations; duplicate units begin serving as named donors.

**After level 14 / level-4 cap:** XP becomes Command Research and duplicate combining stops. Spending shifts to team rules, formation capacity, targeted scouting, trait rewrites, item awakenings, enemy modifiers, and spectacle systems.

**Deep endless:** escalation comes from readable event chains—splits, summons, transformations, chain reactions, boss phases, hazards, and battlefield-wide payoffs—while board cap and UI readability remain bounded.

This is a blueprint, not production balance. The saved odds calibration has only 144 samples and is weakest in the outer probability buckets. The combat-rating model is a budgeting proxy, not a full unit-power estimator. The Monte Carlo model omits player skill, shop variance, composition synergy, and content-specific contract value.

Before implementation, run: (1) larger odds calibration; (2) contract-choice prototypes at chapters 5, 20, and 40; (3) readability tests for chain-reaction fights; (4) price sensitivity around 18–24% of the reference curve; and (5) multi-session save/resume and defeat-reset UX tests.
